conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I have the most accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me start by asking you some questions to assess your eligibility.

Do you have any children under 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 19 years old. He's currently doing his A-levels at sixth form college, so he's still in education.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To continue assessing your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I live in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to ask:

Do you live with Alex, or if he lives elsewhere, do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards his upkeep (such as food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives here at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - his mum claims it for him. She lives with us too.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Just to confirm I have all the information I need:

Since Alex's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him and you all live together, only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child. Are you looking to take over the claim from her, or were you asking about eligibility in general?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I was just wondering if I could claim it instead of her, really. I wanted to understand if I'd be eligible.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Based on the information you've provided, let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news - you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex!**

Here's the summary:

✓ Alex is 19 and doing his A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education (Child Benefit can continue until age 20 for children in approved education or training)
✓ You live in the UK 
✓ Alex lives at home with you, making you responsible for him

**However, there's an important point to note:** Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at any time. Since Alex's mum is currently claiming it, if you wanted to claim instead:

1. She would need to contact the Child Benefit Office to stop her claim
2. Then you would need to make a new claim

You and she would need to agree between yourselves who should claim. If you cannot agree, HMRC will decide who gets it.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and currently doing his A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education. According to the rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. You live in the UK (Leeds) and Alex lives at home with you, which means you are responsible for him. You meet all the basic eligibility criteria: the child is under 20 and in approved education, you live in the UK, and you live with the child. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. However, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child at any time. Since Alex's mum is currently claiming it, if you wanted to claim instead, she would need to contact the Child Benefit Office to stop her claim, and then you would need to make a new claim.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who is 19 and in approved education (A-levels). The claimant lives in the UK with Alex. However, Alex's mother is currently claiming Child Benefit for him. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, so if the claimant wishes to claim instead, the mother would need to stop her claim first, and then the claimant could make a new claim.

You live in the UK (Leeds) and Alex lives at home with you, which means you are responsible for him. You meet all the basic eligibility criteria: the child is under 20 and in approved education, you live in the UK, and you live with the child. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. However, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child at any time. Since Alex's mum is currently claiming it, if you wanted to claim instead, she would need to contact the Child Benefit Office to stop her claim, and then you would need to make a new claim.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who is 19 and in approved education (A-levels). The claimant lives in the UK with Alex. However, Alex's mother is currently claiming Child Benefit for him. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, so if the claimant wishes to claim instead, the mother would need to stop her claim first, and then the claimant could make a new claim.
